abstract: Azabicyclo[2.1.1]hexanes (aza-BCHs) and bicyclo[1.1.1]pentanes (BCPs) have become increasingly important in medicinal chemistry as $sp^3$-rich frameworks. These three-dimensional cores serve as metabolically robust alternatives to flat aromatic rings, helping drug candidates "escape from flatland." Developing methods to directly convert, or "scaffold hop," between these bioisosteres via single-atom skeletal editing would allow chemists to navigate this valuable structural space with precision. Here, we report a strategy to transition from aza-BCH to BCP scaffolds through a formal nitrogen-atom deletion. The approach pairs photochemical [2+2] cycloadditions—used to build multifunctionalized aza-BCHs—with a subsequent deamination step to produce bridge-functionalized BCPs. This sequence provides a streamlined synthetic solution for substituted BCPs that are otherwise difficult to access, offering a modular path to privileged bridged bicycles of pharmaceutical interest.
